entry.title: Everything you need to achieve Enterprise-Wide Integration and iPaaS Consolidation with SAP
entry.content:
1. Introduction – Why Integration Platform Sprawl Is Holding You Back

As enterprises accelerate their cloud journeys, integration has become both more critical—and more complex - than ever. At the same time, AI is transforming how businesses operate, intensifying the need for seamless, real-time connectivity across systems. Yet many organizations face a hidden barrier: integration platform sprawl.

A recent survey done by SAP and the Americas’ SAP Users’ Group (ASUG), with more than 500 respondents, reveals the scale of the challenge. The average enterprise manages around 18 applications requiring integration, with roughly 30 integration points per application. That translates to nearly 600 individual integrations per organization. Compounding the issue, 75% of respondents report using three or more integration tools simultaneously, as shown in a recent study by IDC.

The result is a fragmented and costly integration landscape that is difficult to manage, slows innovation, and increases exposure to security and compliance risks. There is, however, a clear path forward and it starts with simplifying and unifying integration. SAP Integration Suite plays a central role in enabling the unification.

2. The Reality of Integration Platform Sprawl

Most organizations did not end up in a position of integration sprawl by accident. Each integration platform in today's landscape is usually there for a good reason: a company acquisition, a business unit that wanted its own tooling, or there is an ongoing migration. In isolation, each of those decisions made sense. But the challenge is the cumulative effect of integration sprawl:

  • Fragmented visibility: When something breaks, teams need deep institutional knowledge just to know which platform to log into. Troubleshooting becomes slow and expensive.
  • Slowed innovation: Deciding which tool to use for a new requirement can turn into a cross-departmental negotiation - delaying projects before they even start.
  • Security and compliance risk: Every additional platform is another attack vector and another audit burden.
  • Higher total cost of ownership: Licensing, personnel, and operational overhead multiply with every platform - and skilled integration professionals end up managing infrastructure instead of delivering value.

These are not abstract risks. These challenges translate directly into costs, delays, and missed opportunities.

3. The Hidden Costs of Doing Nothing

The challenges above tend to compound over time. Many organizations find themselves sitting on significant "integration debt" - a backlog of undocumented, poorly understood interfaces that carry both technical and business risk.

At the same time, the integration tools many enterprises rely on today were built for a different era where batch processing, file transfers, and mostly on-premises architectures ruled the day. Today's reality looks very different:

  • Applications span on-premises environments, hyperscalers, and SaaS platforms all at once.
  • Business models change in weeks, not years.
  • AI agents are starting to interact with enterprise systems in real time and autonomously.
  • Data sovereignty regulations require precise control over where data flows.
  • Event-driven Architecture becomes more critical to enable business processes in real-time

An integration setup designed for yesterday simply cannot keep up with what the business needs today and tomorrow.

4. What Enterprise-Wide Integration Consolidation Looks Like

Consolidating all integration workloads onto a single, enterprise-grade integration platform delivers benefits across the organization:

  • Unified visibility and control: One place to monitor, troubleshoot, and manage. When something goes wrong, teams know exactly where to look.
  • Streamlined governance: A single, standardized pipeline for promoting changes to production. Governance becomes something you can actually enforce consistently across all teams.
  • Increased agility: No more indecision about which tool to use. Teams move faster and focus on building value rather than managing infrastructure.
  • Cost optimization: Fewer platforms mean lower licensing costs, leaner operations, and people freed up for more innovative work.

Murphy Oil consolidated multiple iPaaS solutions, including SAP Process Orchestration, to SAP Integration Suite. They migrated over 500 interfaces in about a year and achieved a 25% cost reduction for G&A spending. NuSkin saved $300,000 in the first year of consolidation alone, with ongoing annual savings reinvested into new initiatives. And CONA, a North American Coca-Cola bottler, cut its integration runtime costs by more than 50% while dramatically improving monitoring and error handling across its entire bottling ecosystem.

5. Why SAP Integration Suite Is the Right Platform for Consolidation

SAP Integration Suite is built on open standards and designed to meet you wherever you are in your integration maturity. No matter how complex or diverse your landscape is, SAP Integration Suite has the capabilities to connect your entire application landscape. The suite brings together process integration, API management, business-to-business (B2B) and business-to-government (B2G) connectivity, event-driven integration, data space integration (e.g. Catena-X), and support for AI agents in one unified platform. The breadth of integration capabilities matters. Whether you are connecting business applications, exposing SAP S/4HANA data through APIs, managing EDI or e-invoicing, processing real-time events, or calling external AI models like Joule or Gemini as part of an integration flow, SAP Integration Suite handles it all with its comprehensive suite.

For the sixth consecutive year, Gartner has recognized SAP as a leader in the Integration Platform as a Service Magic Quadrant - citing comprehensive coverage of integration styles, deep AI capabilities, and the breadth of content on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

With over 3,800 pre-built integration scenarios, 4,300 APIs for industry leading applications, 600 business events for event-driven use cases, and connectors for every single one of the top 500 enterprise applications, the platform gives organizations a significant head start. Benchmarking with partners and customers has shown up to five times faster go-live and up to ten times lower build cost compared to starting from scratch.

6. Third-Party Connectivity – Reaching Every Corner of Your Landscape

A key question when consolidating integration platforms is whether the system you're consolidating to can actually connect to everything - not just SAP systems, but the full range of third-party applications, cloud services, legacy infrastructure, and partner ecosystems in a real enterprise landscape. SAP Integration Suite offers over 250 connectors out of the box, covering two distinct types.

Technology adapters work at the protocol level. Examples of technology adapters are HTTP/HTTPS, SOAP, OData, SFTP, AMQP, JDBC, and many more. They are generic and reusable across a wide range of systems and applications, giving developers full control over authentication and communication patterns. Any system that exposes a standard protocol can be connected to in this way.

Application-specific adapters go further and are purpose-built for individual business applications like Salesforce, Workday, Shopify, ServiceNow, and many others. These adapters already know the target system's API structure and come with pre-configured operations. Less configuration means faster delivery, fewer errors, and more focus on the business process.

An independent analysis of the top 517 enterprise applications confirmed that SAP Integration Suite has a technology or application-specific adapter for every single one! Connectivity gaps that might justify keeping a secondary platform are, in practice, extremely rare. And, for the rare instances where a customer adapter is required, the SAP Integration Suite Adapter Development Kit (ADK) can be used to develop a customer adapter to meet your specific requirements.

When it comes to integration, connectivity is only the starting point. What organizations also need is the business logic, data mapping, exception handling, and proven patterns that turn a system connection into a reliable, production-grade integration. This is where the integration content on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub makes a real difference. The Hub hosts over 3,800 pre-built integration scenarios - fully realized integration flows that come with end-to-end process logic, data transformations, error handling, security validation, and best practices from real-world deployments. This is not an unregulated marketplace full of slop. Content is curated by SAP's engineering teams or by certified partners who are licensed to contribute and keep packages up to date. Every package comes with a component ID for direct support in the event of issues.

7. Integration Strategy – Getting the Foundation Right

Consolidating onto a single platform is not just a technical exercise. It requires a clear integration strategy. As more workloads move onto a singular platform, traffic increases, processes become more business-critical, and expectations for reliability grow. Your strategy needs to account for this from the start.

SAP's Integration Solution Advisory Methodology (ISA-M) provides exactly the structured foundation you need. It is a platform-agnostic proven framework for mapping your integration landscape, categorizing use cases, and defining the right integration styles for each scenario. It is designed to help teams move from reactive, ad-hoc integration to a deliberate strategy that scales with the business.

Complementing ISA-M, the integration assessment capability of SAP Integration Suite helps organizations take stock of their existing integration landscape. It assists in identifying what is running, what is redundant, what carries risk, and where consolidation opportunities exist. This is the essential starting point for a credible business case and a realistic migration roadmap. Together, ISA-M and the integration assessment capability give integration teams the language to have strategic conversations with business and IT leadership about how integration enables agility, AI readiness, and long-term scalability.

8. Platform Operations – Running SAP Integration Suite at Enterprise Scale

A solid strategy sets the direction, but the operational foundation needs to be equally strong. When a single platform carries the full weight of enterprise integration, it becomes mission-critical infrastructure. How you build, deploy, monitor, and govern integrations matters more than ever. The BTP Guidance Framework provides architectural and development best practices to ensure integrations are built consistently and securely from day one. These guidelines can be actively enforced through tooling, so standards are upheld as the platform grows.

For day-to-day operations, SAP Integration Suite provides built-in monitoring with real-time visibility into message flows and system health. This is extended further through SAP Cloud ALM, adding centralized monitoring, alerting, and health tracking across the full SAP landscape. This level of cross-landscape observability simply cannot be achieved when monitoring is split across multiple platforms.

SAP Integration Suite also supports CI/CD pipelines and DevOps workflows, so integration teams can manage artifacts with the same discipline applied to application development, including versioning, automated testing, and controlled deployment pipelines. This is exactly what you need when the platform is running hundreds or thousands of business-critical integrations at once.

9. Building the Business Case for Consolidation

When making the case for consolidation internally, framing matters. Positioning integration consolidation as a re-platforming project is difficult to sell. Consolidation and re-platforming by themselves do not create business value. Positioning the consolidation around the core principles that affect every IT organization: risk reduction, productivity improvements (through AI), cost reduction.

A few angles worth considering:

  • Integration debt reduction: Undocumented, poorly understood interfaces are a compliance and security risk. Consolidation is an opportunity to clean up the estate and reduce that exposure.
  • AI readiness: The agentic AI use cases that business leaders are asking about require clean data layers, purpose-built APIs, and real-time event-driven connectivity. Building that foundation now is what unlocks AI value later.
  • Governance and COE maturity: ISA-M gives integration teams a structured framework to get a clear picture of the current landscape and put the governance in place to operate at scale.
  • Hard cost savings: In many cases, the savings from shutting down redundant platforms and reallocating people are enough to justify the project on their own - as the Murphy Oil and NuSkin examples show.

The integration landscape is not getting simpler. AI agents, real-time event-driven architectures, composable ERP, and data sovereignty requirements are all adding new demands to what enterprise integration needs to deliver simplification through consolidation.

10. Join Our Webinar Series – See It in Action

Ready to go deeper? We have put together a webinar series that walks you through everything covered in this blog including live demos, real customer stories, and practical guidance from our product and solution experts. Whether you are just getting started or already planning your consolidation, there is a session for you.

 

  1. Rethinking Integration: Building a Unified Enterprise-wide Platform
  2. From Vision to Execution: Building a Scalable Integration Strategy
  3. Achieve Enterprise-grade Operations for SAP Integration Suite

11. What's Next? Here Is How to Get Started

Liked what you read? Below you will find everything you need to take the next step: from exploring the platform yourself to connecting with our team and diving into the content that is most relevant for your journey. We have classified the content into different personas so you can get started at the right place and at the right pace.

How to get started as Integration Strategist 

 

Asset Type

How is this asset useful?

Get started with the SAP Integration Suite website and learn more about iPaaS consolidation. Also, check out our Integration Roadmap to understand future capabilities and features.

New to SAP Integration Suite or just getting started? The product website gives you a solid platform overview, and the Roadmap Explorer shows you what is coming next -  so you can plan with confidence.

Whitepaper on Future-proof integration for cloud ERP  and SAP’s CIO Guide on Integration Architecture Guide

Need to bring leadership along? The Future-proof Integration whitepaper and CIO Guide give decision makers a concise, executive-level view of why modern integration architecture matters - and what to do about it.

Dive deeper with a demo video and our solution brief

See the platform in action and get the key facts in one place. Perfect for sharing with stakeholders who need a quick but concrete picture of what SAP Integration Suite can do.

Get started with the SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology website and learn more about integration strategies in this blogpost: SAP Integration Solution Advisory at a Glance

Consolidation starts with a clear strategy. ISA-M gives you the structured methodology to map your landscape, categorize use cases, and define the right integration patterns - before you start building.

Get enabled on our official Learning Journey: Getting started with SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology to lay the right foundation and start developing with following mission: Get started with Integration Suite, Integration Assesment

Ready to dive deeper on Integration Strategy? The Learning Journey gets you up to speed on ISA-M, while the mission catalog and Integration Assessment help you take stock of your existing estate and build a realistic roadmap.

 

How to get started as Architect

 

Asset Type

How is this asset useful?

Get enabled on our official BTP Guidance framework to lay the right foundation and access more than 100 SAP Learning Journeys for SAP BTP and more

Build on the right foundation from day one. The BTP Guidance framework and Learning Journeys give you the architectural principles and hands-on enablement you need to design integrations that are secure, scalable, and built to last.

Navigate to the SAP Integration Suite service entry page in the SAP Discovery Center

Your one-stop shop for service details, pricing, and hands-on missions. The Discovery Center gives you everything you need to evaluate SAP Integration Suite.

Take a look at the marketecture of SAP BTP and explore SAP BTP reference architectures in the SAP Architecture Center. Here you can also review the BTP solution diagrams for detailed scenario setup.

Stop designing from scratch. The BTP reference architectures and solution diagrams give you proven, real-world blueprints you can adapt to your own landscape - saving time and reducing architectural risk from the start.

 

How to get started as Integration Developer

 

Asset Type

How is this asset useful?

Receive technical insights and documentation on SAP Integration Suite within the SAP Help Portal

Your go-to reference once you start building. When you hit a technical challenge, the SAP Help Portal has the documentation and guidance you need - with dedicated entries for both SAP Integration Suite.

Start developing: Check out the mission catalog and Get started with Integration Suite

The fastest way to get your hands dirty. The mission catalog walks you through real integration scenarios step by step, so you can go from zero to your first working integration in no time.

Develop your SAP skills for free, anywhere, anytime, and at your own pace on the  SAP Learning site

 

Keep your skills sharp at your own pace. The SAP Learning site gives you free access to structured courses and learning paths on SAP Integration Suite - whenever you need them.

Tune in to technology deep-dives in our Devtoberfest series

Go deep on the technical details. Devtoberfest sessions are built by developers, for developers - practical, hands-on, and focused on the topics that actually matter when you are building integrations.

Build and publish integration content on SAP Business Accelerator Hub and leverage published APIs & Events for development

Don't build from scratch when you don't have to. The Business Accelerator Hub gives you ready-to-use integration content, APIs, and events - so you can deliver faster and spend your time on what actually adds value.

How to get started as Operations Specialist

 

Asset Type

How is this asset useful?

Learn how to apply CI/CD for SAP Integration Suite  and review the technical documentation for CI/CD in SAP Integration Suite

Running a consolidated platform at scale means changes need to move through environments safely and consistently. CI/CD gives you the automation and pipeline discipline to do exactly that -  without breaking what is already working.

Learn about concepts of secure communication and apply the highest security standards Explore options for identity and access management in SAP Integration Suite.

When one platform carries all your enterprise integration workloads, security is non-negotiable. These resources help you apply the right standards and keep access tightly controlled from day one.

Run an integration flow under well defined boundary conditions and how to apply design guidelines

 

Good integrations don't just work - they are built to last. Applying design guidelines and running flows under well-defined boundary conditions ensures your integrations are stable, predictable, and easy to maintain at scale.

How to monitor integration flows with inbuilt monitoring capabilities and how to achieve centralized integration monitoring with Cloud ALM

You cannot manage what you cannot see. Built-in monitoring and Cloud ALM give you real-time visibility across all your integration flows - so you can catch issues early and keep business-critical processes running smoothly.

Enable Naming Conventions in integration development

Small things matter at scale. Consistent naming conventions across your integration estate make it dramatically easier to manage, troubleshoot, and hand over integrations - especially as your team and platform grow.

#StayTuned

entry.title: Beyond the Bot: Why Utilities Need “Agentic” AI on SAP BTP to Solve the Grid Complexity Crisis
entry.content:
Beyond the Bot: Why Utilities Need “Agentic” AI on SAP BTP to Solve the Grid Complexity Crisis

 

The Grid Complexity Wall

The utility industry is hitting a wall—and it’s not a small one.

For decades, power grids were predictable. Energy flowed in one direction: from generation to transmission to distribution to the customer. Systems like SAP IS-U were designed around this linear model. Billing cycles were stable, meter roads were periodic, and exceptions were manageable.

That world no longer exists.

Now things change, today’s grid is dynamic, decentralized, and highly unpredictable. Electric vehicles charge at random times. Rooftop solar pushes power back into the grid. Heat pumps create new consumption patterns. Customers are no longer just consumers, they are producers.

This shift has introduced a level of complexity that traditional systems were never designed to handle.

Due to this now?

Utilities are now overwhelmed with:

  • Billing exceptions.
  • Meter data inconsistencies.
  • Unexpected usage of spikes.
  • Data reconciliation issues across systems .

 

Many organizations tried to solve this with automation, especially RPA. But here’s the reality: bots don’t solve complexity. They only automate repetition.

 

A bot can follow a script. It cannot understand context, and that’s exactly where the problem lies.

To manage the “messy grid” of today, utilities need to move beyond deterministic automation. They need systems that can interpret, decide, and act.

This is where Agentic AI comes in.

 

Why a “Messy Core” Kills “AI”

Before we talk about AI, we need to talk about architecture. One of the biggest misconceptions is that you can simply “add AI” on top of an existing SAP landscape. AI is only as effective as the environment it operates in.

Agentic AI systems depend on something critical: observability.

They need to understand:

  • What rules exist.
  • How decisions are made.
  • Where do the data come from.
  • What events are happening in real time.

If your core SAP system is filled with deeply embedded custom logic—user exits, enhancements, Z-programs—then that logic is invisible to AI.

 

It becomes a black box and if AI cannot “see” the rules, it cannot reason.

This is why Clean Core is not just an SAP best practice anymore—it is a foundational requirement for AI.

A clean core ensures:

  • Business logic is transparent.
  • Processes are standardized.
  • Extensions are externalized and visible.

 

This is where SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) plays a key role. A simple way to think about it:

  • S/4HANA is the system that executes transactions reliably .
  • BTP is the layer where intelligence, decisions, and adaptability live

If the core is the engine, BTP is the brain. Without that separation, AI cannot function effectively.

Architecture View: Clean Core + Agentic AI on BTP

 

 

Agentic AI vs. Traditional RPA

Let’s make this real with a common utility scenario.

Scenario: High Usage Billing Dispute

A customer receives a bill that is significantly higher than expected and raises a dispute.

What happened today with RPA?

  • The system flags the anomaly
  • A bot may stop the invoice or trigger a workflow
  • The case is routed to a human agent
  • Investigation is manual, time-consuming, and inconsistent

The bot did its job—but only partially. It identified the issue but could not resolve it.

Now imagine the same scenario with Agentic AI

An AI agent doesn’t just flag—it investigates.

It can:

  • Analyze historical consumption patterns
  • Cross-check weather data for abnormal usage
  • Validate meter health from AMI systems
  • Review distributed energy resource activity (like solar export)
  • Identify whether the issue is behavioral, environmental, or technical

Then it can act:

  • Determine probable root cause
  • Trigger a field service request if needed
  • Adjust billing or flag for human approval
  • Log the entire decision process for audit

The key difference is simple:

Bots automate steps.
Agents handle outcomes.

This shift—from execution to reasoning—is what makes Agentic AI fundamentally different.

The Roadmap to Autonomous Utility

Moving to this model doesn’t happen overnight. But there is a clear path forward.

  1. Event-Driven Foundations

Traditional SAP systems operate in batches. That doesn’t work in real-time grid.

Utilities need to adopt event-driven architecture using services like SAP Event Mesh.

This allows systems to:

  • React instantly to meter events
  • Trigger workflows in real time
  • Enable AI agents to act as events occur

Without events, there is no real-time intelligence.

  1. Externalized Logic

One of the biggest shifts is moving business logic out of the core.

This includes:

  • Billing rules
  • Validation logic
  • Regulatory checks
  • Exception handling

Using CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) on BTP, this logic becomes:

  • Modular
  • Transparent
  • Easier to maintain
  • Accessible to AI systems

This is critical because AI needs structured, visible logic to operate effectively.

  1. Semantic Data Layer

AI is only as good as the data it uses.

Utilities typically have fragmented data across:

  • SAP systems
  • GIS platforms
  • AMI systems
  • Customer platforms

A semantic layer helps unify this data into a consistent model.

This ensures:

  • One version of truth
  • Better context for AI decisions
  • Reduced ambiguity

Without this, even the most advanced AI will produce unreliable results.

Governance: The Guardrails of Agentic AI

With great intelligence comes great responsibility, especially in a regulated industry like utilities.

You cannot allow AI to operate without control.

Key concerns include:

  • Regulatory compliance
  • Billing accuracy
  • Auditability
  • Customer trust

The answer is not to limit AI—but to govern it properly.

This includes:

  • Human-in-the-loop checkpoints for critical decisions
  • Clear approval workflows
  • Full traceability of AI decisions
  • Strong grounding using SAP AI Core

Governance ensures that AI operates within defined boundaries.

In fact, Clean Core governance becomes even more important in an AI-driven world. It ensures that all decisions—whether human or machine—follow a consistent and compliant path.

Conclusion: Survival of the Modern Grid

Utilities today are at a turning point.

The complexity of the grid is not going away. It will only increase.

Organizations that continue to rely on heavy core customization are creating a long-term problem. They are building systems that are harder to adapt, harder to scale, and impossible for AI to understand.

In many ways, this becomes a “digital anchor”—holding them back while the industry moves forward.

On the other hand, utilities that embrace:

  • Clean Core principles
  • BTP as an innovation platform
  • Event-driven architecture
  • Agentic AI

will be in a position to not just manage complexity—but to turn it into an advantage.

The journey doesn’t need to start big.

Start small.

Pick one high-volume exception process—billing disputes, meter validation, or service orders. Externalize the logic. Build it on BTP. Introduce intelligence step by step.

That’s how transformation begins.

Because the future utility is not just automated.

It is autonomous, adaptive, and intelligent.

And that future starts now.

entry.title: How We Built ASTRA Joule Agent for Automation Testing Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

In my experience working on multiple SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementations, one phase that consistently stands out as challenging is testing. No matter how well the system is designed, testing often becomes time-consuming and difficult to manage—especially when dealing with multiple modules like Employee Central, Payroll, and integrations.

During several projects, I observed that teams were spending a significant amount of effort on repetitive manual validation, coordination across systems, and analyzing failures. While automation tools helped to some extent, they still required heavy setup and maintenance.

This led us to explore how we could make testing more efficient and intelligent using SAP BTP and AI capabilities. That’s how we started working on what we now call the ASTRA Joule HR Agent—a solution aimed at simplifying and improving the testing experience.

The Challenge We Faced

From a practical standpoint, testing in SAP SuccessFactors is not just about executing scripts—it involves validating complete business processes and ensuring data consistency across systems.

Some of the common challenges we encountered were:

  • Handling large regression cycles with hundreds of scenarios
  • Managing complex and realistic test data
  • Difficulty in identifying the root cause of failures
  • Constant switching between multiple tools (SuccessFactors, test tools, Jira, etc.)

These issues often slowed down release cycles and required significant manual effort.

Our Approach – ASTRA Joule HR Agent

To address these gaps, we built ASTRA as a centralized, intelligent powerhouse on SAP Business Technology Platform. We leveraged SAP Business AI through Joule skills to create a conversational, "human-centric" testing experience.

Instead of navigating complex menus, users can interact with ASTRA through SAP Build Work Zone. By integrating Joule, we’ve made it possible to manage tests, gain insights into failures, and even trigger executions using natural language.

Key Technical Highlights:

  • Prebuilt Library: We launched with 926+ automation scripts aligned with SAP Best Practices.

  • Full-Stack Integration: Built using the SAP Cloud Application Programming  model, ensuring seamless OData connectivity with SuccessFactors and S/4HANA.

  • AI Orchestration: Using Joule to provide smarter interactions and faster insights into test logs.

Benefits

1. Extensive Module Coverage

ASTRA doesn’t just "test"; it validates the entire HCM lifecycle. This includes Employee Central, Recruiting, Onboarding, Benefits, Time Management, and Performance & Goals. It even handles custom Extension Center objects, ensuring your unique business logic is never left unchecked.

 

 

2. End-to-End & Integration Testing - Enables both functional and integration testing across SAP SuccessFactors, SAP ERP, SAP S/4HANA, and external systems—helping ensure full end-to-end process validation.

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. AI-Powered Insights (Joule Skills)

By utilizing SAP Business AI, ASTRA helps users understand why a test failed without digging through raw logs. Joule assists in identifying failed scenarios and provides intelligent summaries, drastically reducing investigation time.

 

 

 

 

4. Dynamic, Input-Driven Execution

Efficiency is key. ASTRA allows users to enter specific Entity IDs to run focused tests for individual records. The system dynamically filters and executes only the relevant test cases based on your input data.

 

 

 

5. Automated Documentation & Defect Management

  • Traceability: Every execution generates a PDF-based result document for step-by-step audit trails.

  • Jira Integration: Failed scenarios automatically trigger ticket creation in JIRA, ensuring the engineering team is notified in real-time.

 

 

 

6. Joule-Powered Assistance with SAP Business AI (Joule Skills)

  • Uses SAP Business AI through Joule skills to enhance test execution and analysis.
  • Provides intelligent assistance to help users understand test results and failures faster.
  • Helps identify failed scenarios and related logs without manual analysis.
  • Improves overall testing efficiency by reducing time spent on investigation and coordination.

 

 

Business Value

From what I’ve observed while working with this approach, the impact is quite practical and noticeable in day-to-day project execution:

  • Faster Release Cycles: With better visibility into test results and fewer manual dependencies, we were able to move through release cycles with more confidence and less delay.
  • Reduced Manual Effort: A significant portion of repetitive validation was minimized, which allowed both functional and technical teams to spend more time on critical tasks rather than regression testing.
  • Improved Data Reliability: By validating end-to-end scenarios with realistic test data, we could identify issues earlier and avoid unexpected problems during go-live.
  • Better Visibility for Stakeholders: Having centralized dashboards within SAP Build Work Zone made it easier for stakeholders to track progress, understand results, and make informed decisions without relying on multiple tools.

Conclusion

Based on my experience, testing in SAP SuccessFactors projects often becomes a coordination-heavy activity. What worked well for us with this approach was shifting the focus from managing test execution to actually improving system quality.

By leveraging capabilities available on SAP Business Technology Platform along with Joule, we were able to make testing more streamlined and easier to manage.

The ASTRA Joule HR Agent, as we implemented it, is not just about automation—it’s about making testing more meaningful, reducing effort, and helping teams deliver stable and reliable HR solutions more consistently.

entry.title: Introducing ATUL Architecture : A New Blog Series on SAP BTP for Intelligent Utilities
entry.content:
Over the past few years, I’ve had the chance to work closely with utilities that are modernizing their operations using SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, and event‑driven, real‑time architectures. One thing has become very clear: Utilities need simple, practical architecture patterns — not product brochures, not theory, and not marketing slides.

So I’m starting a new blog series called ATUL Architecture, where I’ll share practical patterns, diagrams, and lessons learned from real projects. The goal is simple: Make SAP BTP architecture easier to understand and easier to apply in the utilities industry.

This series is for architects, consultants, developers, and anyone working on SAP‑based utility transformation.

What the Series Will Cover

Here’s the plan for the first six posts:

ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP

  • Clean core

  • Integration strategy

  • Why BTP becomes the “digital backbone”

  • A simple digital‑core diagram

This post sets the foundation for the entire series.

 

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP

  • Event Mesh

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA flow

  • Real‑time vs batch

  • A simple event‑flow diagram

This will focus on how utilities can move from slow batch processes to real‑time operations.

 

ATUL Architecture #3 — Bridging OT and IT: Bringing Grid Data into SAP BTP Safely

  • IEC 61850

  • MQTT

  • Substation → BTP patterns

  • Security considerations

This post will show how OT data can be brought into SAP BTP in a safe and scalable way.

 

ATUL Architecture #4 — AI on SAP BTP for Grid Operations

  • Time‑series data

  • Anomaly detection

  • Predictive maintenance

  • AI → workflow → S/4HANA integration

This will focus on how AI becomes actionable inside SAP processes.

 

ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

  • Data lake

  • Semantic models

  • Governance

  • Analytics for grid + customer

This post will cover how utilities can build a unified data layer on BTP.

 

ATUL Architecture #6 — Intelligent Utility Blueprint: End‑to‑End Architecture on SAP BTP

  • One consolidated diagram

  • End‑to‑end flow

  • Real‑time + AI + automation

  • Business outcomes

This will be the “big picture” post — the one that ties everything together.

I Want Your Input

Before I publish the first post, I’d love to hear from the SAP Community:

  • What topics would you like to see in this series?

  • Are there specific SAP BTP patterns you want explained?

  • Any challenges you’re facing in utilities, integration, or real‑time operations?

  • Should I include more diagrams, code snippets, or architecture trade‑offs?

Your suggestions will help shape the series.

What’s Next

I’ll publish ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP next. It will include a simple diagram and a practical explanation of why BTP becomes the digital backbone for modern utilities.

Looking forward to your ideas and feedback.

entry.title: New Dates for Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator Live Sessions
entry.content:
Since TechEd last year, the certification for SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Administrator is available. It is a system-based assessment, where you complete tasks in an authentic SAP Software environment – a real SAP BTP global account.

Currently, I am delivering the second time the Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator live session track, which introduces you to this new format of hands-on assessments and summarizes the topics covered in the certification.

Because we are seeing a high demand for these live sessions, we just added additional dates for you in July, September, and November in 2026. Please join me in the journey to become an SAP BTP Administrator.

💡Hint:
The core of the Administrating SAP Business Technology Platform learning journey leading to the certification is the Operating SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP200) course. Check-out my blog post summarizing BTP200.

entry.title: New Course Available: Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management
entry.content:
We’re pleased to announce the availability of a new course on SAP Learning:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management.

👉Access the course here:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

This standalone course is designed to help professionals build strong, practical skills in configuring and managing SAP Customer Data Cloud for modern, compliant, and seamless customer identity experiences.

Why This Course?

Customer identity is at the core of trusted digital experiences. Organisations today must securely manage customer data, comply with evolving regulations, and support seamless interactions across channels and devices—all while enabling personalisation at scale.

Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management provides a comprehensive introduction to SAP Customer Data Cloud, focusing on real‑world configuration and administration tasks that directly support customer identity and access management (CIAM) scenarios.

Learning Outcome

By the end of this course, learners will be able to configure and administer SAP Customer Data Cloud to support secure and compliant customer identity management. Learners will design and manage account and data store schemas, including group models; configure site settings using the SAP Customer Data Console; and implement lite and full registration flows, authentication options, and identity journeys using UI Builder, while applying security and governance best practices.

Course Structure

The course is structured into logical units that guide learners from platform context through hands‑on configuration:

  1. SAP Customer Data Cloud in the Modern CX and Regulatory Landscape
    Understand how SAP Customer Data Cloud supports regulatory compliance and how it fits within the SAP Customer Experience portfolio.

  2. Console Site Configuration and Administration
    Learn how to navigate the SAP Customer Data Console, configure essential site settings, and work with customer data.

  3. Designing and Managing Data Schemas
    Design and manage account and data store schemas, edit schema fields, configure advanced data stores, and create group models for organizing customer accounts.

  4. Managing Customer Identity and Access
    Implement lite and full registration flows, manage identity journeys using UI Builder, and configure authentication and security options.

This structure ensures a clear progression from foundational concepts to practical identity and access management scenarios.

Who Is This Course For?

This intermediate‑level course is designed for professionals who work with SAP Customer Data Cloud and customer identity solutions, including:

  • SAP Customer Data Cloud administrators
  • Implementation consultants
  • CX professionals involved in customer identity, registration, and data management

Developer‑specific topics are addressed separately in other courses within the broader CIAM curriculum.

Part of a Growing CIAM Curriculum

This course is the first of four CIAM‑focused courses that will be added to the Learning Journey Implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud on learning.sap.com. Together, these courses will progressively expand the available self‑paced learning content and provide comprehensive coverage of SAP Customer Data Cloud capabilities.

Get Started

Whether you’re implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud for the first time or strengthening your existing expertise, Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management is the ideal place to start.

👉Enroll now:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

Stay tuned—more CIAM courses are coming soon.

entry.title: SAP DMS Client – A Desktop Companion for Cloud Integration Data Archiving
entry.content:
Why I Built This

While looking into Cloud Integration data archiving, I came across Kishor Gopinathan's blog post SAP Cloud Integration – Data Archiving to BTP SDM. It walks through the full setup — creating service instances, building the CloudIntegration_LogArchive destination, and activating archiving via API.

I followed it while onboarding onto a new Integration Suite landscape, and it worked great. But I noticed that the workflow assumes we have a REST client like Postman at hand to call APIs, and some familiarity with CMIS to browse the archived content. Not everyone on the team has that setup ready — and honestly, assembling JSON payloads and fetching OAuth tokens just to activate archiving felt like unnecessary friction for what should be a straightforward task.

So I built a small desktop app to lower that barrier: the SAP DMS Client. I built it with the help of AI tooling, which made it realistic to go from idea to working desktop app within my onboarding timeline. It reads our DMS and Cloud Integration service keys and gives us a UI for managing repositories, exporting destination files, activating archiving, and browsing archived content — no REST client or CMIS knowledge required. The code is open source, so anyone can inspect and adapt it.

What It Does

The app is built with Electron, React, and Material UI (following SAP Horizon design guidelines). Once we load our two service key files — one for DMS, one for Process Integration Runtime — we get four things:

  • Repository management — create, list, and delete DMS repositories without CMIS API calls
  • Destination export — generate a CloudIntegration_LogArchive.json with all credentials pre-filled, ready to import into BTP Cockpit
  • Archiving activation — activate Cloud Integration data archiving in one click (the app handles the OAuth2 token exchange)
  • File explorer — browse the YYYY/MM/DD folder structure that Cloud Integration creates in the repository

The destination export is probably the biggest time saver. Instead of manually extracting clientId, clientSecret, tokenServiceURL, and the SDM endpoint from the service key JSON, the app assembles the full destination file automatically. We just import it into BTP Cockpit and move on.

Here's what the generated file looks like:

{
  "Name": "CloudIntegration_LogArchive",
  "Type": "HTTP",
  "URL": "https://<sdm-endpoint>/browser",
  "Authentication": "OAuth2ClientCredentials",
  "RepositoryId": "<repository-id>",
  "clientId": "<from-service-key>",
  "clientSecret": "<from-service-key>",
  "tokenServiceURL": "https://<auth-endpoint>/oauth/token"
}

No copy-paste errors, no missed fields.

One thing to note: when we import the destination into BTP Cockpit, all fields are populated automatically except the client secret — BTP doesn't include it in the import format for security reasons. We'll need to grab the clientSecret from our DMS service key file and paste it manually into the destination configuration in the Cockpit after import.

What We Need Before Starting

The same BTP services from the original blog:

Service Plan What We Need

Integration Suiteenterprise / standardCloud Integration capability activated
Process Integration RuntimeapiService instance + service key
Document Management Service, Integration OptionstandardService instance + service key

Setup details: Initial Setup for DMS and Creating Service Key for Cloud Integration.

The app is on GitHub at github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client. The easiest way to get started is to download a pre-built installer from the Releases page:

  • macOS — .dmg file
  • Windows — .exe installer

Alternatively, we can build from source with Node.js 18+:

git clone https://github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client.git
cd da-dms-client
npm install
npm run electron:dev

The Workflow in Practice

Once the app is running, the flow follows a specific order — each step depends on the previous one:

  1. Load the DMS service key in Settings — this connects us to our Document Management Service instance
  2. Create a repository — we need a repository before we can generate a destination (the destination includes the repository ID)
  3. Export the destination file — the app generates the CloudIntegration_LogArchive.json with all the values from the service key
  4. Import the destination into the Integration Suite subaccount — in BTP Cockpit under Connectivity > Destinations (remember to fill in the client secret manually after import)
  5. Load the Cloud Integration service key in Settings — this connects us to the Process Integration Runtime instance
  6. Activate data archiving — this only works if the destination already exists in BTP, otherwise Cloud Integration won't know where to write the logs

 

Don't expect logs to appear immediately. As described in the original blog, the log collection process runs on a 7-day cycle — so it may take several days before the first archived files show up in the repository.

After archiving is active, the File Explorer lets us browse what Cloud Integration has stored. The folder structure is date-based (2026/04/15/) with .zip archives of message processing logs for each day.

Conclusion

Kishor Gopinathan's original blog remains the foundation for understanding Cloud Integration data archiving to SDM. The DMS Client just makes the repetitive parts easier so we can spend less time on plumbing and more on the integration itself.

The project is open source at github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client. If you've dealt with Cloud Integration archiving in your landscape, I'd be curious to hear how you've approached it — or if there are other parts of the DMS workflow that could use better tooling. Drop a comment or open an issue.
